title: Quadratic Equation and Inequalities Test - 1 (Topic Test )

questions.questionText: Let $\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{(\tan (x-2))\left(\mathrm{r} x^2+(\mathrm{p}-2) x-2 \mathrm{p}\right)}{(x-2)^2}=5$ for some $\mathrm{r}, \mathrm{p} \in \boldsymbol{R}$. If the set of all possible values of q , such that the roots of the equation $\mathrm{r} x^2-\mathrm{p} x+\mathrm{q}=0$ lie in $(0,2)$, be the interval $(\alpha, \beta]$, then $4(\alpha+\beta)$ equals :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We are given

$$ \lim_{x \to 2} \frac{\tan(x-2)\left(rx^2+(p-2)x-2p\right)}{(x-2)^2}=5. $$

We have to first find the relation between $r$ and $p$, and then determine all values of $q$ for which the roots of

$$ rx^2-px+q=0 $$

lie in $(0,2)$.

Step 1: Simplify the limit

Let

$$ f(x)=rx^2+(p-2)x-2p. $$

Then the given limit becomes

$$ \lim_{x\to 2}\frac{\tan(x-2)\,f(x)}{(x-2)^2}=5. $$

Now, as $x\to 2$,

$$ \tan(x-2)\sim x-2. $$

So the limit becomes

$$ \lim_{x\to 2}\frac{(x-2)f(x)}{(x-2)^2} = \lim_{x\to 2}\frac{f(x)}{x-2}. $$

For this limit to be finite, we must have

$$ f(2)=0. $$

Now,

$$ f(2)=r(2)^2+(p-2)(2)-2p=4r+2p-4-2p=4r-4. $$

So,

$$ 4r-4=0 \implies r=1. $$

Then

$$ f(x)=x^2+(p-2)x-2p. $$

Since $f(2)=0$, factor $(x-2)$:

$$ x^2+(p-2)x-2p=(x-2)(x+p). $$

Hence the limit becomes

$$ \lim_{x\to 2}\frac{\tan(x-2)(x-2)(x+p)}{(x-2)^2} = \lim_{x\to 2}\frac{\tan(x-2)}{x-2}(x+p). $$

Using

$$ \lim_{t\to 0}\frac{\tan t}{t}=1, $$

we get

$$ 1\cdot (2+p)=5. $$

So,

$$ p+2=5 \implies p=3. $$

Thus,

$$ r=1,\qquad p=3. $$

Step 2: Form the quadratic equation

Now the equation becomes

$$ x^2-3x+q=0. $$

We want both roots to lie in $(0,2)$.

Let the roots be $\alpha_1,\alpha_2$. Then

$$ \alpha_1+\alpha_2=3,\qquad \alpha_1\alpha_2=q. $$

Since both roots lie in $(0,2)$, let us find the condition on $q$.

Step 3: Use condition for both roots in $(0,2)$

Let

$$ f(x)=x^2-3x+q. $$

Since coefficient of $x^2$ is positive, parabola opens upward.

For both roots to lie in $(0,2)$, we need:

1. Real roots

So discriminant must be non-negative:

$$ \Delta=9-4q\ge 0 \implies q\le \frac94. $$

2. Both roots positive

Since product of roots is $q$,

$$ q>0. $$

3. Both roots less than $2$

Now check value at $x=2$:

$$ f(2)=4-6+q=q-2. $$

If both roots are less than $2$, then for an upward parabola, point $x=2$ lies to the right of both roots, so we need

$$ f(2)>0. $$

Thus,

$$ q-2>0 \implies q>2. $$

Now combine:

$$ 2

So the set of all possible values of $q$ is

$$ (\alpha,\beta]=(2,\tfrac94]. $$

Hence,

$$ \alpha=2,\qquad \beta=\frac94. $$

Therefore,

$$ 4(\alpha+\beta)=4\left(2+\frac94\right) =4\left(\frac{17}{4}\right)=17. $$

Final Answer

$$ \boxed{17} $$

So, the correct option is Option C.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If the quadratic equation $(\lambda+2) x^2-3 \lambda x+4 \lambda=0, \lambda \neq-2$, has two positive roots, then the number of possible integral values of $\lambda$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For the quadratic equation

$$ (\lambda+2)x^2-3\lambda x+4\lambda=0,\qquad \lambda\neq -2 $$

to have two positive roots, let us use the NCERT conditions for a quadratic equation.

If the roots are $\alpha$ and $\beta$, then for both roots to be positive, we need:

  1. Discriminant $\ge 0$ so that roots are real,

  2. Sum of roots $>0$,

  3. Product of roots $>0$.

1. Identify coefficients

Comparing with $ax^2+bx+c=0$, we get

$$ a=\lambda+2,\qquad b=-3\lambda,\qquad c=4\lambda $$

2. Condition for real roots

Discriminant:

$$ D=b^2-4ac $$

So,

$$ D=(-3\lambda)^2-4(\lambda+2)(4\lambda) $$

$$ D=9\lambda^2-16\lambda(\lambda+2) $$

$$ D=9\lambda^2-16\lambda^2-32\lambda $$

$$ D=-7\lambda^2-32\lambda $$

For real roots,

$$ -7\lambda^2-32\lambda \ge 0 $$

$$ -\lambda(7\lambda+32)\ge 0 $$

Multiplying by $-1$ reverses the inequality:

$$ \lambda(7\lambda+32)\le 0 $$

This gives

$$ -\frac{32}{7}\le \lambda \le 0 $$

So possible integral values from this condition are

$$ \lambda=-4,-3,-2,-1,0 $$

But given $\lambda\ne -2$, so remaining are

$$ \lambda=-4,-3,-1,0 $$

3. Condition for sum of roots positive

Sum of roots is

$$ \alpha+\beta = -\frac{b}{a}=\frac{3\lambda}{\lambda+2} $$

For two positive roots,

$$ \frac{3\lambda}{\lambda+2}>0 $$

Since $3>0$, this means

$$ \frac{\lambda}{\lambda+2}>0 $$

This is positive when numerator and denominator have same sign:

  • $\lambda>0$ and $\lambda+2>0$, or

  • $\lambda<0$ and $\lambda+2<0$

So,

$$ \lambda>0 \quad \text{or} \quad \lambda<-2 $$

From our shortlisted values $-4,-3,-1,0$, this keeps only

$$ \lambda=-4,-3 $$

4. Condition for product of roots positive

Product of roots is

$$ \alpha\beta=\frac{c}{a}=\frac{4\lambda}{\lambda+2} $$

For positive roots,

$$ \frac{4\lambda}{\lambda+2}>0 $$

Again,

$$ \frac{\lambda}{\lambda+2}>0 $$

This gives the same condition:

$$ \lambda>0 \quad \text{or} \quad \lambda<-2 $$

Among the shortlisted values, again we get

$$ \lambda=-4,-3 $$

5. Check both values

  • For $\lambda=-4$:

    $$ D=-7(16)-32(-4)=-112+128=16>0 $$

    So roots are real and distinct.

  • For $\lambda=-3$:

    $$ D=-7(9)-32(-3)=-63+96=33>0 $$

    So roots are real and distinct.

Hence both values give two positive roots.

Therefore, the number of possible integral values of $\lambda$ is

$$ \boxed{2} $$

So, the correct option is B.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If the set of all solutions of $\left|x^2+x-9\right|=|x|+\left|x^2-9\right|$ is $[\alpha, \beta] \cup[\gamma, \infty)$, then ( $\alpha^2+\beta^2+\gamma^2$ ) is equal to:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We need to solve

$$ |x^2+x-9|=|x|+|x^2-9|. $$

We will do it by considering intervals where the expressions inside modulus change sign.

The critical points are obtained from:

  • $x=0$ from $|x|$

  • $x=\pm 3$ from $|x^2-9|$

  • roots of $x^2+x-9=0$, but for NCERT-style interval splitting here, it is enough to use the main sign-change points $-3,0,3$ and then check sign of $x^2+x-9$ in each interval.

Now solve interval-wise.

1. For $x\le -3$

Here,

  • $|x|=-x$

  • $|x^2-9|=x^2-9$

  • Also $x^2+x-9>0$, so $|x^2+x-9|=x^2+x-9$

Thus,

$$ x^2+x-9=-x+(x^2-9) $$

$$ x^2+x-9=x^2-x-9 $$

$$ x=-x $$

$$ 2x=0 \Rightarrow x=0 $$

But $0\not\le -3$, so no solution here.

2. For $-3\le x\le 0$

Here,

  • $|x|=-x$

  • $|x^2-9|=9-x^2$

Now we check $x^2+x-9$ on this interval.

At $x=-3$,

$$ x^2+x-9=9-3-9=-3<0 $$

At $x=0$,

$$ x^2+x-9=-9<0 $$

So throughout this interval,

$$ |x^2+x-9|=-(x^2+x-9)=-x^2-x+9. $$

Hence equation becomes

$$ -x^2-x+9=-x+(9-x^2). $$

Simplify RHS:

$$ -x^2-x+9=-x+9-x^2. $$

Both sides are identical, so this is true for every $x\in[-3,0]$.

So all $x\in[-3,0]$ are solutions.

3. For $0\le x\le 3$

Here,

  • $|x|=x$

  • $|x^2-9|=9-x^2$

Also on this interval,

$$ x^2+x-9 $$

At $x=0$, it is $-9<0$, and at $x=3$, it is $9+3-9=3>0$.

So the sign changes inside this interval.

Let us find the root:

$$ x^2+x-9=0 $$

$$ x=\frac{-1\pm\sqrt{37}}{2}. $$

The positive root is

$$ \frac{-1+\sqrt{37}}{2}. $$

So split further.

(i) For $0\le x\le \frac{-1+\sqrt{37}}{2}$

Here $x^2+x-9\le 0$, so

$$ |x^2+x-9|=-x^2-x+9. $$

Equation:

$$ -x^2-x+9=x+(9-x^2) $$

$$ -x^2-x+9=-x^2+x+9 $$

$$ -x=x $$

$$ 2x=0 \Rightarrow x=0. $$

So only $x=0$, already included in previous interval.

(ii) For $\frac{-1+\sqrt{37}}{2}\le x\le 3$

Here $x^2+x-9\ge 0$, so

$$ |x^2+x-9|=x^2+x-9. $$

Equation:

$$ x^2+x-9=x+(9-x^2) $$

$$ x^2-9=9-x^2 $$

$$ 2x^2=18 $$

$$ x^2=9 $$

$$ x=\pm 3. $$

In this interval, only $x=3$ is valid.

So from $[0,3]$, only $x=0,3$ are solutions.

4. For $x\ge 3$

Here,

  • $|x|=x$

  • $|x^2-9|=x^2-9$

  • $x^2+x-9>0$, so $|x^2+x-9|=x^2+x-9$

Thus,

$$ x^2+x-9=x+(x^2-9) $$

$$ x^2+x-9=x^2+x-9 $$

This is true for every $x\ge 3$.

So all $x\in[3,\infty)$ are solutions.

Complete solution set

Combining all intervals:

$$ [-3,0]\cup[3,\infty). $$

Thus,

$$ \alpha=-3,\quad \beta=0,\quad \gamma=3. $$

Now,

$$ \alpha^2+\beta^2+\gamma^2=(-3)^2+0^2+3^2=9+0+9=18. $$

So the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{18} $$

Hence, Option B is correct.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
Let $\alpha, \beta$ be the roots of the equation $x^2 - 3x + r = 0$, and $\frac{\alpha}{2}, 2\beta$ be the roots of the equation $x^2 + 3x + r = 0$.

If the roots of the equation $x^2 + 6x = m$ are $2\alpha + \beta + 2r$ and $\alpha - 2\beta - \frac{r}{2}$, then $m$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For the equation $x^2 - 3x + r = 0$ with roots $\alpha, \beta$, by using the standard results:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \alpha+\beta=3 \\ & \frac{\alpha}{2}+2 \beta=-3 \end{aligned} $$

The first line comes from “sum of roots” $= -\dfrac{\text{coefficient of }x}{\text{coefficient of }x^2} = -\dfrac{-3}{1}=3$.

The second line comes from the equation $x^2 + 3x + r = 0$, whose roots are $\dfrac{\alpha}{2}$ and $2\beta$. So, their sum is:

$$\frac{\alpha}{2} + 2\beta = -\frac{3}{1} = -3$$

Now solve the two equations:

$$\alpha+\beta=3 \Rightarrow \alpha=3-\beta$$

Substitute into $\dfrac{\alpha}{2}+2\beta=-3$:

$$\frac{3-\beta}{2}+2\beta=-3$$

Multiply by $2$:

$$3-\beta+4\beta=-6 \Rightarrow 3+3\beta=-6 \Rightarrow 3\beta=-9 \Rightarrow \beta=-3$$

Then $\alpha=3-(-3)=6$.

Now use “product of roots” for $x^2 - 3x + r = 0$:

$$\alpha\beta=r \Rightarrow r=6\times(-3)=-18$$

Now consider the equation $x^2 + 6x = m$. Rewrite it in standard form:

$$x^2 + 6x - m = 0$$

So, product of its roots $= \dfrac{-m}{1}=-m$.

The roots are given as $2\alpha + \beta + 2r$ and $\alpha - 2\beta - \dfrac{r}{2}$. Substitute $\alpha=6$, $\beta=-3$, $r=-18$:

First root:

$$2\alpha + \beta + 2r = 2(6)+(-3)+2(-18)=12-3-36=-27$$

Second root:

$$\alpha - 2\beta - \frac{r}{2} = 6-2(-3)-\frac{-18}{2}=6+6+9=21$$

Now product of roots:

$$ \begin{aligned} & =(2 \alpha+\beta+2 \mathrm{r})\left(\alpha-2 \beta-\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow-\mathrm{m}=(-27)(21) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=567 \end{aligned} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $\alpha, \alpha+2, \alpha \in \mathbf{Z}$, be the roots of the quadratic equation $x(x+2)+(x+1)(x+3)+(x+2)(x+4)+\ldots .+(x+\mathrm{n}-1)(x+\mathrm{n}+1)=4 \mathrm{n}$ for some $\mathrm{n} \in \mathbf{N}$. Then $\mathrm{n}+\alpha$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We are given that

the quadratic equation is

$ (x)(x+2) + (x+1)(x+3) + (x+2)(x+4) + \ldots + (x + n - 1)(x + n + 1) = 4n $

and the roots are $ \alpha, \alpha + 2 $ (both integers).

We need to find the value of $ n + \alpha $.

Step 1: Expand the general term

The $k^{th}$ term of the left-hand side is:

$ (x + k)(x + k + 2) $

Expanding this:

$ (x + k)(x + k + 2) = x^2 + 2(k+1)x + k(k+2) $

Step 2: Sum over all $k$ from $0$ to $n-1$

$ \text{LHS} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} [x^2 + 2(k+1)x + k(k+2)] $

Let's compute each part separately.

  1. For $x^2$:

    $ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} x^2 = n x^2 $

  2. For $2(k+1)x$:

    $ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} 2(k+1)x = 2x \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (k+1) = 2x \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = n(n+1)x $

  3. For $k(k+2)$:

    $ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k(k+2) = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (k^2 + 2k) = \sum k^2 + 2\sum k $

Now,

$ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}, \quad \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k^2 = \frac{(n-1)n(2n-1)}{6} $

Therefore,

$ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k(k+2) = \frac{(n-1)n(2n-1)}{6} + n(n-1) $

Simplify:

$ = n(n-1)\left( \frac{2n-1}{6} + 1 \right) = n(n-1)\left( \frac{2n-1+6}{6} \right) = \frac{n(n-1)(2n+5)}{6} $

Step 3: Combine all terms

$ \text{LHS} = n x^2 + n(n+1)x + \frac{n(n-1)(2n+5)}{6} $

The equation is

$ n x^2 + n(n+1)x + \frac{n(n-1)(2n+5)}{6} = 4n $

Step 4: Simplify by dividing both sides by $n$ (since $n > 0$)

$ x^2 + (n+1)x + \frac{(n-1)(2n+5)}{6} = 4 $

$ x^2 + (n+1)x + \left[\frac{(n-1)(2n+5)}{6} - 4\right] = 0 $

Simplify the constant term:

$ \frac{(n-1)(2n+5)}{6} - 4 = \frac{(n-1)(2n+5) - 24}{6} = \frac{2n^2 + 3n -29}{6} $

Thus, the equation is

$ x^2 + (n+1)x + \frac{2n^2 + 3n - 29}{6} = 0 $

Step 5: Use relation between roots and coefficients

Let roots be $ \alpha $ and $ \alpha + 2 $.

Then,

Sum of roots:

$ \alpha + (\alpha + 2) = 2\alpha + 2 = - (n+1) $

so,

$ \alpha = \frac{ - (n+1) - 2}{2} = -\frac{n+3}{2} $

Product of roots:

$ \alpha(\alpha + 2) = \frac{2n^2 + 3n - 29}{6} $

Substitute $ \alpha = -\frac{n+3}{2} $:

$ \alpha(\alpha + 2) = \left(-\frac{n+3}{2}\right)\left(-\frac{n-1}{2}\right) = \frac{(n+3)(n-1)}{4} $

Step 6: Equate the two expressions for product

$ \frac{(n+3)(n-1)}{4} = \frac{2n^2 + 3n - 29}{6} $

Simplify:

$ 3(n+3)(n-1) = 2(2n^2 + 3n - 29) $

Expand:

$ 3(n^2 + 2n - 3) = 4n^2 + 6n - 58 $

$ 3n^2 + 6n - 9 = 4n^2 + 6n - 58 $

Simplify:

$ 3n^2 + 6n - 9 - 4n^2 - 6n + 58 = 0 $

$ -n^2 + 49 = 0 \implies n^2 = 49 $

$ n = 7 \text{ (since } n \in \mathbf{N}) $

Step 7: Find $ \alpha $ and hence $ n + \alpha $

$ \alpha = -\frac{n+3}{2} = -\frac{7+3}{2} = -5 $

$ n + \alpha = 7 + (-5) = 2 $

$ \boxed{n + \alpha = 2} $

Correct Option: C (2)
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{S}=\left\{x^3+a x^2+b x+c: a, b, c \in \mathrm{~N}\right.$ and $\left.a, b, c \leq 20\right\}$ be a set of polynomials. Then the number of polynomials in S , which are divisible by $x^2+2$, is
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$ S=\left\{x^3+a x^2+b x+c: a, b, c \in \mathbb{N} \text { and } a, b, c \leq 20\right\} \text { so } a, b, c \in\{1,2,3, \ldots 20\} $$

$x^3+a x^2+b x+c$ is divisible by $x^2+2$

$$\therefore $$ $ x^3+a x^2+b x+c=\left(x^2+2\right) L(x)$

Let $L(x)=x+n$ because L.H.S is cubic have leading coefficient 1 .

$$ \begin{aligned} x^3+a x^2+b x+c & =\left(x^2+2\right)(x+n) \\ & =x^3+x^2 n+2 x+2 n \end{aligned} $$

Compare $n=a, b=2,2 n=c \Longrightarrow c=2 a$

so, $c=2 a$ and $b=2$

Finding the Number of Possible Polynomials

  • For $b$ : There is only $\mathbf{1}$ choice $(b=2)$, which is within the range $[1,20]$.

  • For $a$ and $c$ : Since $c=2 a$, we must ensure $c \leq 20$.

  • If $a=1, c=2$

  • If $a=2, c=4$

  • …

  • If $a=10, c=20$

    There are 10 possible values for the pair ( $a, c$ ).

Total polynomials $=($ Choices for $b) \times($ Choices for $a, c$ pairs $)=1 \times 10=\mathbf{1 0}$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If $\alpha, \beta$, where $\alpha<\beta$, are the roots of the equation $\lambda x^2-(\lambda+3) x+3=0$ such that $\frac{1}{\alpha}-\frac{1}{\beta}=\frac{1}{3}$, then the sum of all possible values of $\lambda$ is
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
The given equation is $\lambda x^2-(\lambda+3) x+3=0$. We can find the roots by factoring:

$$ \lambda x^2-\lambda x-3 x+3=0 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \lambda x(x-1)-3(x-1)=0 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ (x-1)(\lambda x-3)=0 $$

The two roots are : $ x=1, \quad x=\frac{3}{1}$

We are given that $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots such that $\alpha<\beta$ and the following condition holds:

$$ \frac{1}{\alpha}-\frac{1}{\beta}=\frac{1}{3} $$

Because we don't know if 1 is smaller or larger than $\frac{3}{\lambda}$, we must consider two distinct cases.

Case A : $\alpha=1$ and $\beta=\frac{3}{\lambda}$

Substitute these into the condition $\frac{1}{\alpha}-\frac{1}{\beta}=\frac{1}{3}$ :

$$ \begin{array}{r} \frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{3 / \lambda}=\frac{1}{3} \\ 1-\frac{\lambda}{3}=\frac{1}{3} \\ 1-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{\lambda}{3} \\ 1-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{\lambda}{3} \\ \frac{2}{3}=\frac{\lambda}{3} \Longrightarrow \lambda=\mathbf{2} \end{array} $$

Check the constraint $\alpha<\beta$ : If $\lambda=2$, then $\beta=3 / 2=1.5$. Since $1<1.5$, this value is valid.

Case B : $\alpha=\frac{3}{\lambda}$ and $\beta=1$

Substitute these into the condition $\frac{1}{\alpha}-\frac{1}{\beta}=\frac{1}{3}$ :

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{3 / \lambda}-\frac{1}{1}=\frac{1}{3} \\ & \frac{\lambda}{3}-1=\frac{1}{3} \\ & \frac{\lambda}{3}=1+\frac{1}{3} \\ & \frac{\lambda}{3}=\frac{4}{3} \Longrightarrow \lambda=4 \end{aligned} $$

Check the constraint $\alpha<\beta$ : If $\lambda=4$, then $\alpha=3 / 4=0.75$. Since $0.75<1$, this value is valid.

The possible values for $\lambda$ are 2 and 4 .

$$ \text { Sum }=2+4=6 $$

The sum of all possible values of $\lambda$ is 6.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: The smallest positive integral value of $a$, for which all the roots of $x^4-a x^2+9=0$ are real and distinct, is equal to
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Given equation

$$ x^4-a x^2+9=0--- \text { (1) } $$

let $x^2=t$, equation (1) become

$$ t^2-a t+9=0--- \text { (2) } $$

If $t_1$ & $t_2$ are roots of equation (2) then four distinct roots of equation (1) is $\left\{-\sqrt{t_1},-\sqrt{t_2}, \sqrt{t_1}, \sqrt{t_2}\right\}$

roots of this equation are real and distinct if $\mathrm{D}>0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & (-a)^2-4 \times 9>0 \\\\ & \Rightarrow a^2-36>0 \end{aligned} $$

so $a \in(-\infty,-6) \cup(6, \infty)$

so smallest positive integral value of $a$ is 7 as $a>6$.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
If the domain of the function

$$ f(x)=\log _{\left(10 x^2-17 x+7\right)}\left(18 x^2-11 x+1\right) $$

is $(-\infty, a) \cup(b, c) \cup(d, \infty)-\{e\}$, then

$90(a+b+c+d+e)$ equals:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$ egin{aligned} & ext { } f(x)=\log _{\left(10 x^2-17 x+7 ight)}\left(18 x^2-11 x+1 ight) \ & \log _b a ext { is defined if } a>0 ext { and } b>0 ext { and } b eq 1 ext {. } \ & 18 x^2-11 x+1>0 \ & 18 x^2-9 x-2 x+1>0 \ & 9 x(2 x-1)-1(2 x-1)>0 \ & (2 x-1)(9 x-1)>0 \ & S_1: x \in(-\infty, 1 / 9) \cup(1 / 2, \infty) ext {. } \ & 10 x^2-17 x+7>0 \ & 10 x^2-10 x-7 x+7>0 \ & 10 x(x-1)-7(x-1)>0 \ & (10 x-7)(x-1)>0 \ & S_2: x \in(-\infty, 7 / 10) \cup(1, \infty) ext {. } \ & ext { And } 10 x^2-17 x+7 eq 1 \end{aligned} $$

$$ egin{aligned} & 10 x^2-17 x+6 eq 0 \ & 10 x^2-12 x-5 x+6 eq 0 \ & 2 x(5 x-6)-1(5 x-6) eq 0 \ & (2 x-1)(5 x-6) eq 0 \ & S_3: x eq 1 / 2,6 / 5 \Rightarrow x eq 0.5,1.2 . \ & ext { Final solution } x \in\left[S_1 \cap S_2 ight]-\{0.5,1.2\} . \ & x \in[(-\infty, 1 / 9) \cup(1 / 2, \infty)] \cap[(-\infty, 7 / 10) \cup(1, \infty)]-\{0.5,1.2\} . \ & x \in(-\infty, 1 / 9) \cup(1 / 2,7 / 10) \cup(1, \infty)-\{1.2\} . \end{aligned} $$

Since 0.5 is not included, it need not to be subtracted.

Compare final solution with $(-\infty, a) \cup(b, c) \cup(d, \infty)-\{e\}$.

$$ egin{aligned} & a=1 / 9, b=1 / 2, c=7 / 10, d=1 ext { and } e=1.2 . \ & 90(a+b+c+d+e)=90(1 / 9+1 / 2+7 / 10+1+1.2) \ & =10+45+63+90+108=316 . \end{aligned} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: The number of the real solutions of the equation: $x|x+3|+|x-1|-2=0$ is
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
given $x|x+3|+|x-1|-2=0$.

critical points $x=-3,1$.

two critical points means 3 cases.

Case 1 : $ x<-3,|x+3|=-(x+3)$ and $|x-1|=-(x-1)$. equation become

$$ \begin{aligned} & -x(x+3)-(x-1)-2=0 \\ & -x^2-3 x-x+1-2=0 \\ & -x^2-4 x-1=0 \Longrightarrow x^2+4 x+1=0 \\ & x=\frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{16-4}}{2}=\frac{-4 \pm 2 \sqrt{3}}{2}=-2 \pm \sqrt{3} \end{aligned} $$

since $x<-3$ so $x=-2-\sqrt{3}$ is only solution.

Case 2 : $-3 \leq x \leq 1,|x+3|=x+3$ and $|x-1|=-(x-1)$. equation become

$$ \begin{aligned} & x(x+3)-(x-1)-2=0 . \\ & x^2+3 x-x+1-2=0 . \\ & x^2+2 x-1=0 . \\ & x=\frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4+4}}{2}=\frac{-2 \pm 2 \sqrt{2}}{2}=-1 \pm \sqrt{2} . \end{aligned} $$

since $-3 \leq x \leq 1$ so $x=-1+\sqrt{2},-1-\sqrt{2}$ both accepted.

Case 3 : $$x>1,|x+3|=x+3 \text { and }|x-1|=x-1 \text {. } $$

equation become

$$ x(x+3)+x-1-2=0 . $$

$$ x^2+4 x-3=0 . $$

$x=\frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{16+12}}{2}=\frac{-4 \pm 2 \sqrt{7}}{2}=-2 \pm \sqrt{7}$. Checking positive solution. since $x>1$, so $x=\sqrt{7}-2 \approx 0.645$, so no solution.

so final solution is

$$ x=-2-\sqrt{3},-1+\sqrt{2},-1-\sqrt{2} . $$

Number of solution = 3
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If $\alpha$ and $\beta(\alpha<\beta)$ are the roots of the equation $(-2+\sqrt{3})(|\sqrt{x}-3|)+(x-6 \sqrt{x})+(9-2 \sqrt{3})=0, x \geqslant 0$, then $\sqrt{\frac{\beta}{\alpha}}+\sqrt{\alpha \beta}$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & (-2+\sqrt{3})(|\sqrt{x}-3|)+(x-6 \sqrt{x}+9)-2 \sqrt{3}=0, x \geq 0 \\ & \text { put }|\sqrt{x}-3|=t \\ & \Rightarrow(-2+\sqrt{3}) t+t^2-2 \sqrt{3}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow t(t-2)+\sqrt{3}(t-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(t+\sqrt{3})(t-2)=0 \\ & t+\sqrt{3} \neq 0 \Rightarrow t=2 \\ & \Rightarrow|\sqrt{x}-3|=2 \\ & \sqrt{x}-3= \pm 2 \\ & \sqrt{x}=5,1 \\ & x=25,1 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=1, \beta=25 \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{\beta}{\alpha}}+\sqrt{\alpha \beta}=10 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Option (2) is Correct. } \end{aligned} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: A building construction work can be completed by two masons A and B together in 22.5 days. Mason A alone can complete the construction work in 24 days less than mason B alone. Then mason A alone will complete the construction work in :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$ \text { Let B's time }=x \text { days } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \text { As time }=(x-24) \text { day } \\ & \text { Rate of } \mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{x-24} \\ & \text { Rate of } \mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{x} \\ & \frac{1}{x-24}+\frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{22.5} \\ & \frac{2 x-24}{x(x-24)}=\frac{1}{22.5} \\ & 45 x-540=x^2-24 x \\ & x^2-69 x+540=0 \\ & (x-60)(x-9)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=60 \\ & \text { At time }=60-24 \\ & =36 \text { days } \end{aligned} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of the equation $x^2+2 a x+(3 a+10)=0$ such that $\alpha<1<\beta$. Then the set of all possible values of $a$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \because \alpha<1<\beta \\ & f(1)<0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1+2 a+(3 a+10)<0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5 a+11<0 \\ & a<\frac{-11}{5} \\ & \therefore a \in\left(-\infty, \frac{-11}{5}\right) \end{aligned} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.
